metric_value: Founded 1998 as Linux consulting company; grew to full services firm via OEM demand
notes: DRAFT profile: marked hold status until mid-October 1999

metric_name: Linuxcare venture funding: KPCB first Linux investment
metric_value: Kleiner Perkins Caulfield and Byers chose Linuxcare as its first Linux-related investment
notes: Significant VC validation; KPCB first Linux investment signal

metric_name: OEM endorsement: Dell, IBM, Sun all contracted Linuxcare
metric_value: Dell, IBM, and Sun have publicly sought Linuxcare's technical service and support capabilities for their first Linux offerings
notes: Three major OEM contracts simultaneously; strong market validation

metric_value: Dell contracted Linuxcare for back-end Linux support for OptiPlex, Dimension desktop lines, Precision workstations, and PowerEdge Servers; www.dell.linuxcare.com mirror site
notes: Largest and most visible OEM partnership

metric_value: IBM gave Linuxcare contract to support Linux on the RS/6000 line
notes: Key IBM partnership

metric_value: Sun signed Linuxcare to provide support for Star Office suite (acquired from German Star Division)
notes: Note: IBM/Sun mirror sites flagged for verification in draft

metric_value: TurboLinux tapped Linuxcare to provide services and support to its North American customers
notes: Linux distributor partnership

metric_value: Linuxcare supports all Linux hardware platforms: Alpha, Intel, MIPS, PowerPC, Sparc, StrongArm
notes: Agnostic hardware support as differentiator

metric_value: Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum support packages varying in response time and coverage; also backline support
notes: Full enterprise support portfolio

metric_value: 24x7 call center, Linuxcare University (training), Linuxcare Labs (certification/benchmarking), Linuxcare Labs (porting/migration)
notes: Four service pillars of Linuxcare strategy

metric_value: Aberdeen finds Linux adoption among enterprise firms starting to accelerate overall; Linux not a flash in the pan
notes: Aberdeen bullish assessment of Linux enterprise trajectory

metric_value: Within the next year (by 2000), IT managers will have a Linux-based alternative in every major application area
notes: Aberdeen prediction of Linux's application coverage breadth

metric_value: Linux enterprise application coverage expanded significantly by 2001. By 2001, Oracle, SAP, IBM DB2, and major enterprise applications all had Linux ports. Linux server revenues grew 63% from 2001 to 2002 to $2 billion, reflecting strong enterprise adoption. However, Linuxcare itself was acquired by Turbolinux and eventually rebranded as Levanta.
notes: Verification of 2000 Linux application breadth prediction | Linux enterprise application breadth expanded substantially by 2001 as predicted; Oracle, SAP, IBM DB2 all on Linux. Linuxcare acquired by Turbolinux 2001. Source: https://www.wired.com/2000/05/linuxcare-cuts-jobs-nixes-ipo/; https://www.serverwatch.com/servers/turbolinux-acquires-linuxcare/; https://www.cnet.com/tech/tech-industry/ibm-dell-win-in-losing-server-market/

metric_value: Freeware business model means buyers get support they paid for: little to none; enterprise OS with no support is a non-starter
notes: Core market opportunity rationale for Linuxcare's business

metric_value: Andrew Tridgell, original author and current team leader for Samba, is a Linuxcare guru
notes: Open-source credibility signal; top-tier Linux contributor

metric_value: Paul Mackerras, leader of Linux port to Power Macintosh and Fujitsu multicomputer platforms, is a Linuxcare guru
notes: Open-source credibility signal for PowerPC platform support

metric_value: Densa Techno Tokyo K.K. (Hitachi subsidiary) partnering with Linuxcare for Japanese Linux support market
notes: International expansion signal

metric_value: Compaq Computer certified DeskPro product line through Linuxcare Labs
notes: Hardware certification program gaining traction
